key_insight: For one-dimensional convex Lipschitz optimization, the stochastic subgradient method’s last iterate achieves the optimal O(1/√n) error with standard fixed stepsizes under i.i.d. bounded-variance noise, but can degrade to (log n)/√n without the i.i.d. assumption.
engineering_relevance: This result clarifies when simple fixed-step stochastic subgradient schemes are safe to use in noisy optimization pipelines and when additional assumptions or averaging are needed to avoid hidden logarithmic slowdowns.
method: Analyze the last iterate of SsGM for fixed horizon n with stepsize η=Θ(1/√n).
Prove an O(1/√n) optimization-error bound under additive i.i.d. subgradient noise with uniformly bounded variance.
Construct a counterexample showing (log n)/√n behavior when the noise is not i.i.d.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports worst-case optimization-error rates for the last iterate, comparing the improved O(1/√n) guarantee against a counterexample with (log n)/√n scaling.
watch_item: The favorable rate depends on the i.i.d. bounded-variance noise model; relaxing independence can reintroduce a logarithmic penalty even in dimension one.
paper_title: New Bounds for the Last Iterate of the Stochastic subGradient Method
